title: Reversible Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List

statement.markdown:
You are given a doubly linked list and you need to sort it in reverse order using a reversible merge sort algorithm. A reversible merge sort is a sorting algorithm that is similar to the standard merge sort, but it produces a new array instead of modifying the original array. In this case, you will need to return a new doubly linked list with the elements sorted in reverse order.

You should also note that the merge function should be reversible, i.e., it should be able to put the original doubly linked list back together in its original order.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of input will contain the number of elements in the doubly linked list, and the second line will contain the elements of the list (space-separated).
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the elements of the sorted doubly linked list (space-separated).
statement.notes: The doubly linked list will be represented as a list of nodes, where each node contains an integer value and a reference to the previous and next node in the list. The input list will be empty or non-empty, but in any case, it will contain at least one node. The merge function should be reversible.

examples.explanation: The doubly linked list [1, 3, 2, 4] is sorted in reverse order, and the output is [4, 3, 2, 1]

hints.text: Think about how you can reverse the sorted doubly linked list in a reversible merge sort algorithm.

editorial.title: Editorial: Reversible Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The key insight here is to realize that the merge function should be reversible, i.e., it should be able to put the original doubly linked list back together in its original order. This requires some creative thinking and problem-solving skills.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach to this problem would be to first sort the doubly linked list using a standard sorting algorithm, and then reverse the sorted list. However, this approach would have a time complexity of O(n log n) because of the sorting step.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach would be to use a reversible merge sort algorithm, which can sort the doubly linked list in reverse order in O(n log n) time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution to this problem is to use a reversible merge sort algorithm, which can sort the doubly linked list in reverse order in O(n log n) time complexity. The key insight here is to realize that the merge function should be reversible, i.e., it should be able to put the original doubly linked list back together in its original order.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for this solution can be shown by induction on the size of the input list. The base case is when the input list has only one node, in which case the sorted list is simply the input list. The inductive step is when the input list has more than one node, in which case we can split the list into two halves, sort each half recursively, and then merge the two sorted halves.
editorial.dryRun: Here is a step-by-step example run of the solution:
commonMistakes: Mistake 1: Using a standard sorting algorithm instead of a reversible merge sort algorithm.
Mistake 2: Not realizing that the merge function should be reversible.

solutions.python:
import sys

class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, x):
        self.val = x
        self.next = None

class Solution:
    def sortList(self, head):
        if head is None:
            return None

        dummy = ListNode(0)
        dummy.next = head
        current = head
        previous = dummy
        
        while current:
            
            if (current.next and current.next.val < current.val):
                
                if (previous == dummy):
                    head = current.next

                
                else:
                    previous.next = current.next
                    current.next = current.next.next
                    previous.next.next = current
                    current = current.next
                    previous = previous.next
                    previous = previous
                    
                
                current = current.next
                current = current
                    
                # Reversed
                # previous.next = current
                # current.next = previous
                
            
            else:
                
                previous = current
                
                current = current.next

        
        # Merge sorted
        dummy.next = self.merge(dummy.next, head)
        return dummy.next

    
    def merge(self, left, right):
        
        dummy = ListNode(0)
        current = dummy

        while left and right:
            if left.val < right.val:
                current.next = left
                left = left.next
            else:
                current.next = right
                right = right.next
            current = current.next

        
        current.next = left if left else right
        return dummy.next

solution = Solution()

for i in range(int(sys.stdin.readline().strip())):
    sys.stdout.write(str(solution.sortList(int(sys.stdin.readline().strip().split()))).strip('[] ').replace(' ', ''))
    sys.stdout.write('\n')
